CURRENT AFFAIRS.

“Tt (the Convention) pronounced—as we counselled
and anticipated it.would—decidedly and unanimously
against the Irish Council Bill.”” So said Mr. Murphy’s
‘“Independent.”” Well, what next for this organ of
Humphrey’s Dump? The  Ha’penny Dreadful ”’ has
come out as an organ of opinion, and in the excitement
. of a trying moment Mr. William Murphy has appa-
rently forgotten that one man’s ha’penny is as good as
another man’s ha’penny. In its leading article the
day after the Convention Mr. Murphy’s paper
declared :—*‘ Animated solely by a patriotic resolve, we
felt it our duty as a Nationalist organ to tell Irishmen
honestly and outspokenly what we thought of the
_measure, etc.”, An emused public will smile when
ey learn that the ‘“ Ha’penny Dreadful,” in its deal-

with the now pitchforked Bill, was ‘‘animated
% Who ever suggested that

ely by patriotic resolve !’

path”; it has endeavoured, moraover, to tempt Irishmen to wander
from the high road of nationality into a narrow, deceptive by-path
50 strewn with brambles in the nature of mischievous checks and
restrictions, wounding alike to self-respect and patriotic feeling,
that they could probably never, had they allowed themselves to be
tempted, have emerged successfully from the cramped and irritat-
ing situation in which it was sought to place them.

That sounds very well if one could forget for the
moment the organ that was blowing the tune. So the
‘“ Ha’penny Dreadful’’ chides people for deviating
from the “bold path!”  Mr. Murphy, in a Robert
Emmet costume, is rather comic, certainly!

But what next for the ‘“ Independent?’ Now that
the Bill has been kicked out, what does it propose to
do? As far as we can see it does not propose to de
anything; it ‘“ hopes,”” and ‘‘ hoping,”’ like talk, is
cheap enough. It says:—‘‘ We hope the effect of the
verdict pronounced by the Convention may be to induce
the Liberal Government to reconsider and to recast its
plan of grappling with the Irish problem.””  Indeed!
There is a great lead, quite an independent lead, in
that! But if the ‘‘ Liberals’’ reconsidered their plan
of grappling with the Irish problem the result would
be an amended Bill. But the Convention kicked out
the Bill without troubling to state what sort of a Bill
they would accept. Does the ‘‘ Independent’” ‘‘hope’
that the Liberals will amend the Bill?

Mr. Murphy’s paper winds up:—

Let us havo a consolidation and unification of the national forces
upon a broad basis; at tho samo timo pressing our demand for
roform vigorously but not immoderately. Thus wo shall achiove-—
soonor, porhaps, than may bo expeoted—tho roalisation of our
cherished aspirations.
And thus the organ of Humphrey’s Dump, finding the
Bill kicked out, attempts to answer the question—what
noxt !—by writing raimeis. Let us press our demand,
‘““bubt not immoderately!”” No fear, Mr William
Murphy will do anything rash in the agitating line!
The phrase, ‘‘ sooner, perhaps, than may be expected,”
is merely a rather awkward change on our old friend
‘“at no far distant date.’’ The organ of Humphrey's
Dump—a ‘ Nationalist_organ,” as it styles itself,
and ‘‘ animated solely by a patriotic resolve’'—has
helped to kick out the Bill without an attemuvt af
amending it; but it ‘‘hopes’’ that the ‘ Liberal ™
Government ‘‘ may ’’ recast its plan of grappling with
the Irish problem. And, turning up the whites of its

X

‘!015,’,9?“, it breathes a ‘“let us have a consolidation,
ete,” and let us press forward our demand, ‘“ but not
immaderately.” That sort of thing appears to bo all
&he answer that the *‘ Independent’’ can give to the
gue{ﬁmm—what next? We wonder what answer will
it give: the guarantors of Humphrey’s Dump, that is
foisted’ on the people, when they are asked to pay up
for the slump !

A% the General Election the “Liberals™ had the
fielp of the Irish:vote in England. The ‘¢ Liberals,”
or some of them, have given pledges not to introduce a
Home Rule Bill in this Parliament. But Ireland was
led %o expect that a substantial instalment leading up
%o: Home Rule would be introduced, and the Irish
Party walked the floor of the British House of Commons
watclied by an expectant and hopeful country.  The
BT fias been brought forth, and, on the motion of Mr.
ghlm Redmond, it has been strangled. We are not
Toterested in the question whether Mr. Redmond was
moved to strangle the Bill because he was afraid of the
people, or because he did not see his way to make the
Best of it. The Bill is dead, anyway. The last para-
graph of the long resolution proposed by Mr. Redmond
Teads as follows :—

ud we call upon the Irish Party o oppose the Bill in the House
af Commons, and to press upon the Government with all their
strength and power to introduce a Measure for the establishment
»f & native Parliament with a responsiblo Executive having power
oxer all purely Irish affairs; and at this crisis in the fortuncs of
Teelznd we invite all the Nationalist forces of the country to unite
In suppost of our representatives in Parliament so as to enable
ifiens to effectively press for a speedy and genuine settlement of the
Trish Question.

Well, if that is not humbug we don’t know what hum-
bug is. The ““ Liberals ”’ got the support of the Irish
vote in England without the Party being pledged to
Home Rule; the Party has kept the people in humour
by the prospect of an instalment worth taking; the in-
stalment has come, and has raised a howl of indigna-
tior, and Mr. Redmond then turns round and asks the
Tonvention to call upon the Party to ‘‘ press the Govern-
ment with all their strength and power” with a view
¥o getting Home Rule! - It is well they woke, certainly!
Surely we must take it that the Party was pressing
with all their strength and power before they got Bir-
rell’s Bill; how is that pressure of strength and power
%o'squeeze a full Parliament out of the Liberals? ‘‘ The
20 far distant date ’’ having at last produced Birrell’s
proposals, which the Convention kicked out with scorn,
not deigning ever to attempt suggestion for amending
them, the Convention unanimously, and with loud and
prolonged cheering, gave a mandate to the Party to
do another indefinite “‘ no far distant date ’’ movement.

The Party having failed to get a Bill worth taking,
Baving succeeded in only getting one that was regarded
as an imsult, and that was kicked out with contempt,
they asked—and what is stranger still, they received—
a fresh blank cheque to try again. Truly, we are a
‘hopeful people.

Mr. Redmond stated at the Convention that he had
reasonr to know that with regard to the constitution of
the body no amendment would be accepted, and he did
not believe amendments could be got on all the other
bad points of the Bill. Under these circumstances
kicking out the Bill was an intelligible procedure, but
if everr satisfactory amendments could not be expected
in fhis little Bill, what prospects did the Convention
see in unanimously deciding to send back the Party to
the floor of the MHouse to get—not amendments, but a
whole Parliament?

THE LEADER.

June 1, 1907.

Mr. Redmond was not very convincing in reply to
his own question—what of the future? It is very easy
to say, as Mr. Redmond: said, that Ireland is strong
enough if she chooses to compel a settlement, of this
question ; you could as easily say that Ireland is strong
enough if she chooses to reduce the drink bill from thir-
teen millions to thirteen hundred pounds. But 1f_ she
doesn’t choose! Again, if Treland were really umte'd?
But does Mr. Redmond think that he is going to unite,
the ¢ Trish’® Times,” the ‘“ Belfast Newsletter”’ and
the ‘ Northern Whig,”” to go no further, and _the
¢« Freoman’s Journal?’  What is the use of talking
about if Ireland were united when we know. there are
two races—one roughly a subject race, and one roughly
an Ascendancy race—in the country, and that the
presence of these two races is at the bottom of all the
trouble? If the subject race refuses to fight the Ascend-
ancy to a finish on the soil of Treland, well then the
only alternative that we can see is a ““ deal.”

Father Humphreys apparently did not get much of
a hearing at the Convention. When he used the words
““the Catholics of Treland’’ ‘‘ voices’’ shouted ‘‘the
Nationalists.”” Indeed, one has sometimes to be a man
of exceptional courage these days in Treland to publicly
refer to the Catholies at all. But when Mr.
Devlin, of Belfast, got up and mentioned the name
of Mr. Sloan, late of the Belfast Custom House steps,
there was loud applause! Evidently Father Hum-
phreys was quite accurate when he said that there were a
large number of tolerance-provers at the Convention.
The eloquent Mr. Devlin orated in the approved
““standing here to-day '’ style; he referred to Mr. Red-
mond, of whom it is now quite cruel to speak, in terms
of exaggerated praise, as ‘‘ the leader of the Irish race
here to-day.”” He referred to himself as ‘“an Irish
Nationalist who, in Irish politics, knows no creed!”
That may be eloquent, for we see that it was greeted
with cheers, but nevertheless it is utter nonsense. Mr.
Devlin is a Catholic who knows no creed in Irish poli-
tics! What would one think of an orator who said that
he was an honourable man, but that he knew no
honesty in business? Mr. Devlin deprecated ‘‘ the in-
troduction of that sectarian spirit which has been the
curse of our country.” Well, why does he not fight it
in Belfast, where Mr. Sloan, whose name the Conven-
tion cheered, comes from?

Some students, etc., of Trinity College have formed
a Gaelic Society for the promotion of the Study of the
Irish Language, Literature, Art, Archwmology, History
and Economics—for short, we suppose we may call the
new society the G.S.S.I.LL.L.A.A.TI.E.T.C.D. The
chair, at the meeting, was occupied by a grandson of
William Smith O’Brien, Professor Gwynn. Professor
Gwynn, Smith O’Brien’s grandson, was recently on a
tour in the land of the Big Brother in the interests of
Trinity College. The Provisional Secretary, Mr. W.
J. Weir, stated that some people read in the name
““ Gaelic ”” something horrible, and he had been aceused
of trying to push forward a Iome Rule Bill cu the
students!  Well, the people to whom Mr. Weir re-
ferred—presumably persons connected with the College
---are even more ignorant and silly than we would have
expected, and we don’t expect much from the class of
people connected with Trinity College.  Mr. Weir
stated that the G.S.S.I.L.L.A.A.TT.E.T.C.D. was non-
political. A Mr. J. M. Fitzgerald, speaking at the
meoting of this ““ non-political *’ society, said that a
very serious danger lay before the College in the next
few months by what he might call their inveterate
enemies! IMighly ‘“non-political,”” eh! This Mr. J.

. Fitzgerald further stated,

‘as reported:— That
d the College was mis-
Judged. Tt was true that the College had been brought
to book for what was called tlie want of national $pitit
—apart from all politics and religious views. Lot them
1ot be an English University, but a great, and the only,
Irish University, and he felt that it would be through
the means of this Society that this object will be
attained.” We have italicised a few words. So tho
~College is to be made the ““only Trish University,”’
and it would be through the means of this non-politi-
cal ” society that Mr. Bryce’s schieme and othor machi-
nations of the ‘“ inveterate enemy ”’ should be defeated,
and that the discredited College is to be hammered into
the ‘“ only Irish University.” Professor Gwynn, even
though he has been spouting in the land of the Big
Bx‘otl.ler, and even though he is a grandson of Smith
O’Bn.en, may not find the green people as simple as
he thinks. A ““ non-political *’ society to help to estab-
lish the monopoly of the discredited College as the
““only Trish University "’ is not slim enough even to
deceive the ““ mere Trish.”

General Sir William Butler is a man that all Trish-
men are proud of ; it is a pity that his great abilities,
as in_the case of so many other Irishmen of parts—the
late Lord Russell of Killowen, for instance—were not
expended in the service of his country rather than in
the service of the British Empire. But even the most
fanatic anti-enlisters must feel proud of the sturdy
general, whose history in connection with the Boer busi-
vess will be remembered when the very names of the
British Jingoes of that time are forgotten. General
Butler has won fame with the pen as well as with the
sword. Well might it be said—cursed be those circum-
stances that deprive Ireland of the services of soms of
the calibre of Sir William Butler. We have tin-pikers
on our side, but circumstances as a matter of fact—let
who likes diagnose the case—drive the real pikers into
the service of the British Empire.

A few days ago the editor of this paper received a
letter marked ‘‘ personal ’’ from General Sir William
Butler, which he has since received permission to pub.
lish. The letter is as follows:—

‘“ Bansha,
“20th May, 1907.

‘“ Dear Sir—You were good enough to send me, some
time ago, a copy of your work, ‘ The Philosophy of Irish
Ireland.” T was about to start for South Africa at the
time the book reached me, and it is only now I have
been able to give it serious study.

‘“May I say to you that I have not met with any
work upon modern Ireland which gives the reader an
analysis of the conditions existing in Ireland to-day so
penetrating, so close, and so true. You have in these
one hundred and fourteen pages of your book laid bare
the very soul of the problem.

““Yours faithfully,

‘“W. F. BUTLER.
“D. P. Moran, Esq.”’

We suppose real soldiers all over the world have much
in common, and the decided sentences of this brief
letter remind one of the crack of the rifle—no qualifica-
tion or indecision about them. We freely confess we
value highly this unlooked-for letter. General Butler
is not afraid to speak his mind on this book, and that
is more than can be said of others, whose *‘ independent
thinking ’’ appears to be very much curtailed by fear
of the cliques. Ireland, so far as the greater part of
its press, and, indeed, of its public men on all platforms
are concerned, would truly appear to have received the
gift of speech in order to conceal its thoughts. General
Butler 18 not afraid of his shadow, as apparently are
some of our great ‘‘independent’’ thinkers. Ireland
would he the better of a great many morc men of the
sturdiness of Sir William Butler.

THE LEADER. o

This week we are glad to be able to welcome 3¢t
another néw Irish industry. Almost exactly six ‘yeare
ago—to be precise, in our issue of May 18th, 19015
published an article on Furniture in our ‘‘Natioh:
building ”’ series. In the course of that article we re-
marked :=— About fifty years ago the furniture trade
of Ireland was at its height, nearly all the furniture
used in the land was of native make.”” Well, the Navax
Saw Mills and Furniture Factory, Ltd., Navan, is fo%
in full swing. It is no amateur or halting effort. The
best and most approved machinery, costing abbit
£6,000, has been laid down, and there are already forly
men working in the furniture factory. The wages bill
at present is about £100 a week, and the capacity ¥
the factory about £200 to £300 in gocds, at wholesgle
prices, weekly. In the course of ordinary develop-
ment there will be one hundred men employed later on-

The making of this particular class of furniture marke
a new industry in Ireland, as it deals with goods fdr
the supply of which we were previously altogéther
dependent upon England.  Good, serviceable and well-
designed suites, drawing-room and dining-rootn, it
mahogany, walnut and oak are turned out. Mr. Michaél
O’Dea, of the well-known Stafford Street firm, is im-
terested in the new enterprise, and so are Messra.
M‘Cann, sons of the late Mr. James M‘Cann, whose
death was such a severe blow to the Irish industrid¥
revival.

Quite needless to say, the new Navan industry has
come to face competition, and to win on its merits. We
would ask all those of our readers who are in the house-
furnishing, hardware and kindred lines, and who deal
in the retail furniture trade, to communicate with the
Navan factory, and get catalogues and particulars. The
recent Birrell Bill that was kicked out at the Conven-
tion dealt with an annual revenue of about £4,000,000,
and the Bill, as we understood it, purported to give @
grant—out of our own money—of more than half a
million per annum. Now with the help of our readers
we would like, within a few years, to add, say.
£4,000,000 annually to the value of the manufactured
products of Ireland. Such a four millions would be in-
comparably superior to even four millions if the latter
came as a free gift from America or elsewhere, for o
would stand for skill and energy and industry.

We are fully conscious of the great Irish grievance on
the head of over-taxation—it is somewhat about three
millions ; unfortunately, however, we cannot at present
redress the wrong, but that is all the more reason why
we should attempt to add at least about three millions
to our productivity. This Navan industry is real business;
and we would like to see it a great success for the sake
of those whose enterprise initiated it, and more so for
the sake of the Irish Industrial Revival.

The raising of early potatoes for the cross-Channel
markets—an industry which, owing to the initiative and
encouragement of the Department of Agriculture, 'has
been carried on at suitable centres in Ireland for a num-
ber of seasons past—promises to prove extremely sue-
cessful this year. Intelligence to hand shows that not-
withstanding the harshness and severity of the late
spring months, the tubers are now coming to maturity
fast, and growers are in keen expectation of profitable
yields. It is satisfactory, moreover, to note that im
Counties Cork, Wexford, and Sligo the area devoted e
this crop this year marks a decided increase over last
season, and there can be little question if satisfactory
prices are realised for the present crop, the industry will
assume even more extensive proportions in those dis-
tricts where the soil and aspect are especially adapted
for the purpose.

An excellent sample of tubers from the Clonakilty
district reached Dublin this week, and raising of the
crop at this centre will, it is understood, be started
within the next few days. Both here and at Youghsl

the light sandy soils which prevail afford a favourable
degree of warmth for the rapid development.of the earlx

varioties planted. As a token of the keen interest
which cross-Channel merchants take in the progress of
the crop, it may be mentioned that a number of English
and Scotch potato buyers visited both districts last week,
and were agreeably impressed with the forward condi-
tion of the tubers being grown. Through the assistance
of the Department of Agriculture, the initial difficulties
of transit and methods of consignment with which the
pioneers of the industry had to contend have been re-
moved, and under present arrangements growers are
enabled to forward their produce to the leading cross-
Channel markets with confidence and despatch. It is
to be hoped that the returns which accrue to growers
this year will place the Irish early potato-raising indus-
try on a wider and more permanent basis than it has
occupied up to the present.

Several Trish societies in London—namely, the Gaelic
League, the Gaelic Athletic Society, Cumann na
nGaedheal, the Dungannon Club, and the National
Council—have issued a joint manifesto denouncing the
Tnternational Exhibition. So far as the Irish Parlia-
mentary Party is concerned, Mr. John Redmond has
written the Conference pointing out that the attitude
of that Party and himself has all along been one of
condemnation of the Exhibition, and the Secretary of
the United Irish League of Great Britain, on the in-
structions of his Standing Committee, has written in
similar terms. In the course of the manifesto we read—
‘“We unanimously and unhesitatingly have come to
the conclusion that the sole effect of this Exhibition
will be to put back the present National Industrial
Revival ; to kill such Irish industries as are weak, and
to weaken those that are strong; to benefit the foreigner
and the foreign carrying companies That therefore
the Exhibition is one which no Irishman nor woman
who has the prosperity of Ireland at heart should, for

- a moment, nor to the extent of a single penny, support.

And we accordingly call upon our countrymen and
women in London to refrain from, in any way, helping
or patronising an Exhibition which can have nothing
but an evil effect on the future of Ireland.”

We assume that this manifesto was sent to the ‘‘ In-
dependent ’ as it was sent to us and other Irish papers.
Up to the time of writing we have not seen any refer-
ence to it in Mr. Murphy’s paper, which recently
mildly startled us all by describing itself as a Nationa-
list organ.

The Irish Revival has left its mark on the hat and
cap industry in Ireland. We have referred before to
its progress as seen in the development of various firms
both in Cork and Dublin. We are glad to refer on this
occasion to another Irish enterprise in the headgear
line—not, we may say, by way of introduction to the

THE LFADER.

JuNE 1, 1907.

Irish Ireland public, for Messrs. D. J. Slattery and Co.,
Jervis St., Dublin, have long since become familiar to
the purchasers of Irish hats and caps—but to congratu-
late the firm on the advance they have made since start-
ing a year ago. It is pleasant to hear that in the past
twelve months the number of hands employed by Slat-
tery and Co. has been largely and continuously aug-
mented, and the present rate of going points to further
large increases. =~ The firm commenced operations by
manufacturing gentlemen’s caps and tweed hats, the
material used being Irish tweed. They were not long
under weigh until their scope also included the various
requirements of ladies in the cap line. No sensible man
would think these days of starting a business that could
not face competition, and we need hardly say that
when Mr. Slattery opened his factory he had carefully
gone into that matter. The firm asks for the support
of Irish men and women because their goods are Irish,
and, as their development shows, they have received it
in very large measure; but they lay special emphasis on
the fact that their goods, as well as being Irish, can
compete in quality and price with any imported pro-
ducts. They say, indeed, that their business motto, is
not ‘“ as good and as cheap,’’ but ‘‘better and cheaper.”’
An industry run in this spirit should have a big ex-
pansion before it.

Have the Post Office authorities any say in the matter
of the wages given to assistants in Post Offices? We
take the following advertisement from the ‘ Belfast
News Letter *’ :—“ Post Office.—Wanted, immediately,
experienced Girl for Post Office and Business; country-
trained assistant preferred; first-class testimonials re-
quired ; salary 5s., indoor. Apply, R. H., 13486, this
Office.”” Salary 5s. a week indoor! Will the Post
Office authorities endeavour to find out who is advertis-
ing with this heavily-laden bait of £13 a year? Why in
Dublin you cannot gct a competent general servant at
the money. But in this case it is not a general servant
that is required, but an experienced girl at Post Office
work, and experienced at business as well, and she
must have ‘‘first-class testimonials,”’ too!

The state of Belfast is truly deplorable, as the evidence
before the L.G.B. Commission indicates. The Chair-
man asked :— Can you suggest to us why these people
do not complain at the City Hall?”’ The witness re-
plied :—*‘ The tenants say they are afraid to make com-
plaints to the sanitary authorities, because if they do,
they are served with notices to quit by the agents.”
Another witness stated that one-third of the Belfast
milk came by rail. The means provided by the railway
companies for transit were far from desirable. He had
seen milk coming in fish vans, the stench from which
was very bad. He had also seen sheep and carcases
being conveyed in the same vans. Questioned by Col.

of manure was a common sight in the city, and was
extremely objectionable. When the bacteria got into
the barrels it was absolutely impossible to get them out.
Evidently with all the ‘“ civic virtues ’’ Belfast is rather
an unsavoury spot.

_What has the religion a person professes got to do
with his efficiency as a drapery salesman? Messrs. J.
Henderson and Co., Omagh, and Kirkpatrick, Allen,
Ritchie (business firms, or whatever these names stand
for), of Cavan, evidently could answer this question if
they wished, for in the ‘‘ Belfast News Letter’’ of a
recent date we find the following two advertisements :—
‘“J. Henderson and Co., Omagh, require a smart Junior
Salesman to the Woollens and Ready-mades.  State
experience, religion, salary (indoor). Drapery and
Boots.—Wanted, smart, pushing Salesman ; good at in-
troduction.  Apply, giving reference, religion, terms,
(indoor), Kirkpatrick, Allen, Ritchie, Cavan.’”’

Sunday, 9th June, has been fixed for the great annual
demonstration of the forces of Irish Ireland in Baile
"~ Atha Cliath, known as the Language Procession. It has
- been hitherto held each year in March, but the un-
favourable nature of the weather on the last two occa-
sions decided the promoters on altering the date to a
more promising season. It is accordingly expected that
this year the demonstration will reach larger proportions
than any previous one. Trusting they may have season-
able weather, those who are arranging the procession
are essaying higher flights than ever before. The
branches will represent an ordered series of Irish his-
torical tableaux, which are being prepared upon a very
elaborate scale.
anything that has ever been seen in processions in
Dublin. In addition all the usual features of the pro-
cession will be included. The temperance, industrial,
athletic, educational, philanthropic and municipal sec-
tions are expected to appear in greater strength than
before. Everything points to an exceptionally fine
turn-out. The Great Southern, Midland, and other
railways are running cheap excursions to Dublin for
_Language Sunday.

A real Irish Exhibition and Féte is being held this
week at the Father Mathew Hall, Church Street. One
of the objects of the Exhibition is to reduce the debt on

. the great and practical temperance enterprise carried
on at Church Street. We have not heard that there
are any imported waiters at this National Exhibition,
but a staff of Irish speakers are in charge of the Irish
Tndustrial Section, in which amongst the many exhibits
are Williams and Wood’s Jams, sweets, etc.; Dripsey
Woollens, George Perry’s boxes, Gill and Son’s books,
Catholic Truth Society’s books, P. O’Reilly and Son’s
boxes, O’Brien and Co.’s mineral waters, Hayes and
Finch’s candles, Dixon’s soaps, Hopkins and Hopkins’

" jewellery, etc., Malone Bros. blacking, etc., Ryan’s
soaps, J. O’Neill’s Lucania cycles, Art Companies
various exhibits, Varian’s brushes, Paterson’s matches,
Crean’s soaps, Goodbody’s tobacco, cigars, and cigar-
ettes; J. Ryan’s fibrous plaster, O’Dea’s (Stafford
Street) Irish-made furniture, Dolphin’s Barn bricks,
Elliott’s poplin ties, etc. There is no Dump about this
Adona¢ na Dealtaine, and we hope that as many of
our readers as possible will pay it a visit during the
remaining days of the week for their own amusement
and for the sake of the Temperance Cause and the In-
dustrial Revival.

land sends us an account of what one man can do im
the way of operating on consumption. O‘ff correspon-
dent, in his covering letter, writes:— Dear Mr.
Editor,—I think that the enclosed will go to show the
truth of your theory, viz., that the people of Ireland
have it within their power to improve their material
welfare very much without asking leave of the English
Government. You may publish the facts enclosed, but
not under my name.’’

His story is as follows:—‘‘I beg to give you some
facts which may be useful, and which you may publish
in your invaluable paper if you think fit. The facts
are these :—Some four or five years ago, several branchez
of the Gaelic League were started in these parts, and
among the first members was a young man, born in thie
country, but of Irish parents. This young man ‘pub
himself heart and soul into the work, and has acquired
a very fair knowledge of the Irish language. Bub he
has done more, he has encouraged and helped on others
to do likewise. And now let me state what he has done
for Irish industry. He got about a dozen other men to
go with him to one shop and ask for Carroll’s tobacco.
The shopkeeper said he did not stock it, but they re-
fused to take any other, and -called again and again
until they got the article required. Having succeeded
in this shop, they did likewise in all the shops in town,
and so got a big sale for one Irish article even outside
Ireland. About twelve months ago he went to live in
Ireland in some remote part in the West, and let us
see what he has done there. He took up his residence
in the village where his fair young wife had been born.
In this village there is one shop, and only one. He
sent to the shop for the household requirements and
demanded the Irish article—matches, soap, etc. The
man in the shop said he never heard that such articles
were made in Ireland. He told him plainly tha¢ he
would not deal with him unless he could supply Irisk
manufactured goods. Within a week he was able to
get in this same shop all the Irish-made goods
he needed. He then went through the houses in the
village and country, and explained to them that the
Irish article was cheaper and better in every way than
the foreign one. He approached the P.P. and got him
to speak to the people on the matter, and so within
twelve months this same shopkeeper sold £600 or £700
worth of goods of Irish manufacture, who, previous to
that, did not sell one shilling’s worth. He also started
a language class, got it into the schools, and got the
Rosary and other prayers recited in the dear old tongue
in the church to the extreme joy of the old people who
still are in possession of that priceless treasure. Such is
the worth of one simple and unassuminng man for faith
and fatherland. May God reward him a thousand fold
for his labours.””

y ARLTAMENTARY action has slumped badly;
P operations on ‘‘ the floor of the House '’ by the
present members, anyway, are at a discount, and pro-
phesies as to what may be done for the evicted tenants
and other causes are taken now with large lumps of
salt. Having bungled the political measure, we have,
and we think with good reason, lost faith in political
predictions regarding other favours to come. IFowever,
if the Party as a force has slumped, let us hasten to
say that it is not quite annihilated; bits of bread may
yet be gathered, and further British thieving and in-
justice may be thwarted somewhat by action on ‘‘the -
floor of the House.”” One can even still talk seriously, if
pessimistically, of what little may be done in Parlia-
ment, but the nation-making suggestion that has
emanated from the collective wisdom of Mr. Martyn’s
Party to go to ‘ the floor of the Hague’’ is only a sub-
ject for laughter; as pantomime, it would be extrava-
gant. After all, the Party can, as a right, walk ‘ the
floor of the Fouse;"’ but even Edward Martyn himself
-——whom a wit has aptly called Edward VIII.—will not
find his credentials from Alderman Cole, John Sweetman
and W. E. Shackleton sufficient to carry him beyond the
first hall-porter leading to the Hague Conference. As
the Parliamentary Party are weak and in sad ease,
Edward VIIL.’s party lose interest in sympathy, for the
latter party were only mildly interesting so long as the
Parliamentary Party were a force. When John Red-
mond flops it follows as a conseauence of the law of
gravitation that Edward Martyn, who was clutching at
his heels and attempting to bite his calves, flops also.

We are back, as it were, where the LEeaper started,
with the exception that a good deal of the propaganda
of the Leaper is accomplished.  The Industriel Re-
vival, the Language Revival, the Temper-
ance  Revival, and the Thought, Revival
have made real progress during the last seven
years. We are alive now to the national bearing of
such things as the railways. The Department has done
something also, though in relation to what it might
have done, and to the money spent upon it, it has been
a failure up to this. The politicians having failed, the
Bill on the “mno far distant date’ having matured,
and having been referred to drawer, we practically re-
sume where we partially left off, and we must now do
our best to use the Home Rule we have in the living
present.

Irish politics, or rather politics in Ireland, concerned
as they are with getting power from a foreign power,
are very distracting. Politics in Ireland arc a neces-
sary distraction, but a distraction nevertheless. The
energies of Irish Ireland have been partly distorted and
partly suspended during the past few years by palitical
prospects and political speculations. The country was
led to expect something worth taking, something worth
going on with, from the present Parliament, and as a
consequence there was a good deal of marking time in
various and important matters. A man expecting a .
legacy in a year or two would find it difficulé to keep
plodding away at some prosy occupation if he could
avoid ibt. Expecting a measure leading up to Home
Rule we were all more or less inclined to mark time.
‘We have been deceived. The Party did not squeeze much
of a proposal out of the ‘‘ Liberals,”’ and such as it
was they, wisely or unwisely, flung it back in the faces
of the ‘“Liberals’’ without as much as condescending
to attompt an amendment. The Bill is dead now, and
the Party are discounted. ~The expected legacy has
not come off, so we all had betfey lose- no delay, but
buckle to again, and address owvselves to the various
odd jobs at hand without being unduly distyacted by
the further prophesies or promises of ouwr ineffective
politicians. ~ Whether Mr. Sexton is or is not on speak-
ing terms with Mr. John Redmond, whether M.
O’Brien is spending wisely oy unwisely his six farthings
on the Continent, and such like other problems are not

In concluding our notice of this volume it is only
right to say that our review gives only an incomplete
idea of the full scope of the work. It has not been
possible to deal with the author’s views on Anglicisa-
tion, the Gaelic League, the Co-operative Movement,

etc.—all of which subjects he treats in a particularly
ablo manner.

ever, is involved, and the incompleteness of his informa-

if anything from the value of his work. M. Dubois’
task was a difficult one; it is only the truth to say that
he has accomplished it with conspicuous success. He
has brought to his subject a large comprehension of its
many aspects; and the abundance of detail which
accompanies his treatment of the various questions is
evidence of a marvellous industry. In fine, M. Dubois

one of the most valuable contributions to the study of

Supposing, then, you are the common or small-garden

leaving of your own accord (unless the landlord offered

It will be for the County Court Judge to fix the

The Disturbance Provisions are simpler. A business

the first shock Protestants themselves will thank us.
They will admit our manhood, and treat us no longer
as slaves whom they can kick at will and with perfect
assurance of impunity. E. CARROLL.

P.S.—Since writing the above I have read another
and a fuller report of what Father Humphreys said at
the Convention. If sectarianism means, as I take it,
attacking people solely on account of their religion, I
can see no ground whatever for the charge made against
him. Te was better justified almost than anyone else
in criticising Mr. Birrell’s Bill, for that Bill would
deprive him of a right that was conceded to all and
cveryone who were not clergymen. It would have laid
him under a disability for no cause but that he was a
priest. T don’t suppose priests would care much tao
exercise this particular right, but all the same there is
no reason why he should sit down quietly, while an
Englishman or anyone else ruled him out of it. Indeed,
he would be a poor-spirited man who would submit, to a
degradation of this kind, unless he had some high
natural or supernatural mwotive to urge him or super-
natural end to serve. As for his attack on Protestants,
it was not on account of their religion, but on account
of their invasion of other people’s rights. From this
fuller account of the Convention’s proceedings, I can
anly conclude that its treatment of Father Humphreys
was even less worthy than I suppesed. It is now per-
fectly clear that he was in no way guilty of sectarianism,
and it is also equally clear that Mr. Joseph Devlin and
the Convention are guilty of sheltering sectarianism.

It is a pity that scarcity of funds is causing the Feis

TTER reading Opomanna’s graphic descrip-
tion of the ‘“Irish Conquest of England,” I
thought perhaps it would be well if an antidote were
provided against the policy of wholesale invasion sug-
gested by that apostle of Gaelic Imperialism.

When John Bull annexes territory he is prepared to
hold it against all comers—black, white or yellow ; if he
is not certain of being able to do so he wastes no
energy, physical or other, in trying to obtain possession.

Trom the manner in which your correspondent beats
the big drum one would think the G.A.A. was the
master of John Bull and his island fortress; il..lld tl_m.t
Sedtan Durve, in his fear of provok.ing a conﬂwt'wu;h
such formidable opponents, had decided 1ot to ‘hmder
any of their attempts at territorial aggression. The un-
employed of West Ham and other parts of London have
heen occasionally in the habit of annexing disused open
spaces, and trying to grow cabbages and other succu-
lents therein, but they have in every case been obl}gcd
in the end to quit, not, however, without a determined
and often protracted resistance. I very much fear our

CGiaclic expansionists belong to the same school of settlers
as these, the difference between them being only one of
degree.  The unemployed believe they have -a moral
right to those spaces, but the others make no such
claim. ITence when both are warned off by the consti-
tuted authorities, the former hold on all the tighter
while the latter abandon their position without further
negotiation.

It is commonly reported that many of the foremost
fighting units of "Opomanna’s army of occupation stand
in awe of a London policeman, and whenever that most
unaggressive individual, finding them trespassing, lifts
his hand and points towards the frontier their colonis-
ing ardour dies forthwith, and without a word of remon-
strance they abandon for ever their much-vaunted con-
quest. To parody Sancho Panza—‘ A territory that
cannot be defended against one policeman isn’t worth
two thraneens,” and "Opomanna must certainly
have got hold of a most original theory of “Colonial
expansion ”’ if he thinks that the new Gaelic conquest
under the conditions described is a fit subject for flag-
weaving.

The work of opening up these possessions is carried
on under great difficulty, and is not by any means the
sort of gala performance that "Opotnanna would
lead us to believe. If I could be sure that readers of
the Leaper would feel interested in the deeds of daring
and bold adventure which mark the progress of some
of these colonising expeditions T should be glad to fur-
nish a detailed description thereof, but I fear lack of
space and public interest are against the project. In
one of the settlements referred to a party of about two
hundred, more or less, boys and Caitin1 thought to
hold a seily on a fine summer Sunday afternoon. The
official piper led off in most promising style, and a dance
was started. Park ranger appeared on the scene.
Music prohibited on the Sabbath. Secretary tried to
bounce him—no use; tried to bribe him—incorruptible.
They were ordered to move on, which they did, the piper
leading in full blast, and the keeper hanging on to their
flanks. He kept them on the move, and gave them no
rest, until the girls were ready to drop from exbaustion,
and then they went home.

The average Cockney has a sort of racial antipathy
to the caman, especially on the Sabbath. He is generally
a peace-loving citizen, and will rather than provoke a
collision often make concessions even to those who may
have taken gratuitous possession of his territory. You
may sometimes hear the remark—‘‘ God love me, if
they would only wash their clubs sometimes I might
be able to put up with them.”” But that is neither here
nor there. They do show fight, however, occasionally.
It is recorded that some time ago during a hurling
practice on Sunday in one of the spaces annexed by our
energetic settlers, the Nonconformist element were stung
into action.  They surrounded the players, and after
the usual mutual exchange of courtesies the encounter
commenced. The Gaels were few in number, but they
were steeped in the battle traditions of Cuchullain and
John L. Sullivan, while their opponents had little to
recommend them but their numbers and a sprinkling of
walking sticks.  Against these latter instruments of
petty warfare the Gaels were armed with camans—ideal
weapons in a skirmish. Unib after unit of the Saxons
went under, the camans waging fierce and bloody war-
fare on their defenceless heads. Tere one of the Gaels
lies prostrate with a Saxon holding him down and an-
other giving him the finishing touches. Not, however,
for long, for into the danger zone with Achillean stride
rushes a comrade, and with double sweep of the unerring
caman delivers to cach a wallop that lays them low and
humble. Reinforcements are coming up in aid of the
Saxon, but the defence is maintained with unusual
stubbornness in spite of the fact that the enemy have
